coredata.dc:title: Preparation of sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots: Applications in food dyes detection, solid-state luminescence and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Meng
Yang, Lijuan
Wang, Dandan
Tao, Yongqing
Wang, Luchun
Wang, Junji
Liu, Shaochi
Quan, Tian
Ke, Famin
Zhang, Kailian
Li, Xiang
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: As diseases such as cardiovascular disease and cancer caused by food problems are more and more frequent, food safety has received great attention. Among them, the safety problem caused by food dyes is more prominent. Thus, it is of great value to develop sensitive detection methods for food dyes. In present study, sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots (namely as R-CDs) had been manufactured by using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as precursor, sulfuric acid as additive for the first time. The structural and fluorescence properties of R-CDs had been systematically studied. The results demonstrated that R-CDs showed uniform spherical morphology and had a graphite-like structure, for which the average diameters size was 5.05 nm. Due to the various functional groups such as hydroxyl, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and -C-SO4, R-CDs emitted bright red fluorescence. Importantly, because of the interactions between the functional groups of R-CDs with the selected food dyes, three dyes including amaranth, brilliant blue FCF and methylene blue can sensitively quench the fluorescence of R-CDs through IFE and static quenching effects. The linearity ranges of them were separately detected as 0.20 μM −20 μM, 10 nM-1 μM and 60 nM-8 μM. The limits of detection (LODs) of them were 70 nM, 4 nM and 20 nM, respectively. Further, R-CDs was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of three dyes from various food samples. To maximize the fluorescence properties of R-CDs, a R-CDs/PVA composite gel was fabricated to make R-CDs fluoresce in solid state condition. The potential of R-CDs/PVA composite gel for preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes was studied. Finally, ascribed to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the potential of R-CDs as probe for cell imaging was explored preliminarily.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-Codoped
Red emissive carbon dots
Food dyes
Solid-state luminescence
Visualization analysis
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of R-CDs/PVA composite gel and preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes
Preparation and characterization of the R-CDs
Fluorescence responses of R-CDs to three food dyes
Exploring the fluorescence quenching mechanisms for the detection of three dyes
Detection of three selected dyes in real samples
Detection of amaranth, bright blue FCF in beverages
Detection of methylene blue in real fish sample
Preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes based on R-CDs/ PVA composite gel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. TEM image of R-CDs. B. XPS survey spectrum of R-CDs. C. C1s spectrum of the R-CDs. D. N1s spectrum of the R-CDs. E. O1s spectrum of the R-CDs. F. S2p spectrum of the R-CDs. G. FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs.

A. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with different excitation wavelengths (Insets showed the R-CDs solution under visible light and under UV light of 365 nm).C. Optimization of the concentration of R-CDs. D. Optimization of the concentration of NaCl. E. Optimization of the incubation time. F. The influence of the metal ions on the fluorescence of R-CDs. G. The influence of the other dyes on the fluorescence of R-CDs.
Selectivity of R-CDs for (A and D) Brilliant blue FCF, (B and E) Amaranth, (C and F) methylene blue in the presence of metal ions (1 mM) or other dyes (10 μM).

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with amaranth at different concentrations. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with brilliant blue FCF at different concentrations. C. Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with methylene blue at different concentrations. D. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of amaranth. E. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of brilliant blue FCF. F. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of methylene blue.

A. Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs and R-CDs with amaranth. B. Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs and R-CDs with brilliant blue FCF. C. Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs and R-CDs with methylene blue. D. Stern-Volmer curves of amaranth at different temperatures. E. Stern-Volmer curves of brilliant blue FCF at different temperatures. F. Stern-Volmer curves of methylene blue at different temperatures.
A. HPLC chromatogram of watermelon flavored soda drink which mainly contained amaranth. B. HPLC chromatogram of blue cocktail which mainly contained brilliant blue FCF.

A. Image of the R-CDs /PVA composite gel under sunlight. B. Image of the R-CDs/PVA composite gel under the UV lamp. C. Image of the R-CDs/PVA composite gel treated with water for 10 min under the UV lamp. D. Image of the R-CDs/PVA composite gel treated with amaranth for 10 min under the UV lamp. E. Image of the R-CDs/PVA composite gel treated with brilliant blue FCF for 10 min under the UV lamp. F. Image of the R-CDs/PVA composite gel treated with methylene blue for 10 min under the UV lamp.
A. The cytotoxicity of R-CDs. B. Image of cells in bright field. C. Fluorescence image of cells after treated with R-CDs for 4 h.
Analysis of amaranth and Bright blue FCF in real beverages by using the N-CDs based fluorescence method and HPLC method (n = 6).
Analysis of methylene blue in real fish sample by using the R-CDs based fluorescence method (n = 6).
Analysis of methylene blue in real fish sample by using HPLC method (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Preparation of sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots: Applications in food dyes detection, solid-state luminescence and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: M. Tian and L.J. Yang contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) were successfully prepared by using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as precursor, sulfuric acid as acid additive for the first time. R-CDs can be applied for the food dyes detection and cell imaging. By construction of R-CDs/PVA composite gel, the luminescence property of R-CDs can be kept and the gel can be used for the preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes.

As diseases such as cardiovascular disease and cancer caused by food problems are more and more frequent, food safety has received great attention. Among them, the safety problem caused by food dyes is more prominent. Thus, it is of great value to develop sensitive detection methods for food dyes. In present study, sulphuric acid-mediated N,S-codoped red emissive carbon dots (namely as R-CDs) had been manufactured by using N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as precursor, sulfuric acid as additive for the first time. The structural and fluorescence properties of R-CDs had been systematically studied. The results demonstrated that R-CDs showed uniform spherical morphology and had a graphite-like structure, for which the average diameters size was 5.05 nm. Due to the various functional groups such as hydroxyl, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and -C-SO4, R-CDs emitted bright red fluorescence. Importantly, because of the interactions between the functional groups of R-CDs with the selected food dyes, three dyes including amaranth, brilliant blue FCF and methylene blue can sensitively quench the fluorescence of R-CDs through IFE and static quenching effects. The linearity ranges of them were separately detected as 0.20 μM −20 μM, 10 nM-1 μM and 60 nM-8 μM. The limits of detection (LODs) of them were 70 nM, 4 nM and 20 nM, respectively. Further, R-CDs was successfully applied to the sensitive detection of three dyes from various food samples. To maximize the fluorescence properties of R-CDs, a R-CDs/PVA composite gel was fabricated to make R-CDs fluoresce in solid state condition. The potential of R-CDs/PVA composite gel for preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes was studied. Finally, ascribed to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the potential of R-CDs as probe for cell imaging was explored preliminarily.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of life requirements and living standards, food safety has received more and more attention. Among them, the safety problem caused by food dyes is very prominent. In particular, colorants and animal dyestuffs (e.g. fishery drugs) are the two most likely to cause safety problems [1,2]. As food additives, colorants are often used to improve the appearance of food, making it more attractive [3]. Food colorants can be split into natural colorants and synthetic colorants according to their sources. Nevertheless, due to the complex preparation process, high preparation cost, poor stability, natural colorants are gradually replaced by the synthetic colorants because they have low price, outstanding stability and bright color [4]. For examples, various synthetic colorants such as amaranth, brilliant blue FCF are usually employed as important additive in food, especially beverages, to brighten their color [1,5]. For another, animal dyestuff such as methylene blue has been effectively used as the chemotherapeutic agent in the aquaculture industry, especially for fish [6]. Although these dyes show high application potential and value in the food field, they also have many potential threats. They are known to be chronically toxic, fatal, carcinogenic and mutagenic [7,8]. In foods such as beverages and fish, excessive levels of these dyes can easily lead to their accumulation through the food chain and into the human body, posing serious health risks. Consequently, it is necessary to develop simple, reliable and efficient methods for the determination of synthetic food dyes in food samples.

To date, various detection methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [10], fluorescence detection method [11], voltammetric determination method [12], electroanalytical sensor [5] and so on have been employed to the detection of these food dyes. Among them, fluorescence detection method has been widely concerned due to the advantages such as fast detection speed, high sensitivity and low cost [13]. Particularly, fluorescence detection method based on fluorescent materials such as carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots and upconversion nanoparticles has attracted more and more attention because of the superiorities in sensitivity and selectivity [14-16].

CDs, one kinds of fluorescent nanomaterials with outstanding fluorescence property, good biocompatibility, photobleaching properties, simple preparation procedure and high water solubility, have obtained wide attention recently [17,18]. According to the different fluorescence color of CDs, they can be divided into blue, green, yellow, orange, red CD, etc. In particular, the red CDs demonstrate low photodamage and high tissue penetration [19,20], and thus they are widely used in the field of fluorescence sensing and imaging [21,22]. Unfortunately, due to the complex luminescence mechanism of red CDs, they are still difficult to prepare, and no universally applicable synthesis method has been developed. Previously, acid mediated synthetic strategy has been effectively used for the synthesis of red CDs by us [23]. In this strategy, by introducing sulfuric acid into the reaction, the degree of polymerization and carbonization of CDs can be controlled by regulating the pH of the reaction solution. In other words, H+ in acid can act as catalyst to promote dehydration reaction, leading to defects in the framework of CDs. As a result, the band gap of CDs becomes narrower. Thus, acid mediated synthetic strategy can be effectively used to synthesize red CDs [24,25]. On the other side, the introduction of heteroatoms such as S can also increase the possibility of red shift of fluorescence emission wavelength [26]. Hence, sulfuric acid-mediated synthetic strategy can be used to prepare red CDs which can be used for food dyes detection. Regrettably, most of the researches have focused on the degradation of dyes [27-29], studies on the fluorescence detection methods which rely on the CDs for the detection of dyes especially for food dyes are still lacking, and the detection method based on red CDs has rarely reported. Consequently, the development of a red CDs based fluorescence detection method for food dyes detection is of great significance.

N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine (Purity > 98%), amaranth (>98%), brilliant Blue FCF (analytical standard), methylene blue (>98%), malachite green (>98%), methyl blue (>98%), sunset yellow FCF (>95%), eosin Y (>90%) and lemon yellow (>95%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (98%) was obtained from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Rhodamine B (>99%) was obtained from Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and was gained from Glpbio. Reagents about metal ions were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd.

Electric blast drying oven (Yamato DKN412C, Japan) was applied to give high temperature reaction conditions for teflon-lined Teflon-lined autoclave for the preparation of R-CDs. The fluorescence property of R-CDs was measured by using a Perkin Elmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. The UV–vis property of R-CDs was carried out through an UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). The morphology and structural characteristics properties of R-CDs were measured using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100F), IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) and K-Alpha + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo fisher Scientific). The fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs were obtained by using a Perkin Elmer FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer. HPLC was conducted on an Agilent 1290 system.

R-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine as raw materials. In details, 0.10 g of N-phenyl-o-phenylenediamine was added into 10 mL of deionized water containing 2 mL of sulfuric acid. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. The products were collected by centrifugation at 7 000 rpm for 10 min to remove the unreactant or overreacted products. Further, the suspension was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 30 min and filtered with a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Next, the filtrate was neutralized with NaOH until neutral. The collected neutral filtrate was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MW: 1000 Da) for one week. Finally, the dialysate was steamed with a rotary evaporator, and the concentrated solution was dried in a vacuum drying oven to obtain the purified black solid sample (R-CDs).

Amaranth and bright blue FCF are often used as food coloring for beverage staining. Thus, blue cocktail and watermelon flavored soda (all purchased at the local supermarket) were separately used as actual samples for R-CDs on the detection of bright blue FCF and amaranth. In order to evaluate the effectiveness of fluorescence method, HPLC method was used for comparison. For HPLC detection, bright blue FCF and amaranth solutions with different concentrations were prepared by using pure water as solvent. HPLC method was established and the standard curve was drawn. Then, the concentrations of bright blue FCF and amaranth in the actual samples were determined by HPLC and calculated according to the standard curve. Finally, the concentrations of bright blue FCF and amaranth in the actual samples were determined and calculated by fluorescence method. The relative deviation was calculated by comparing the actual sample concentration calculated by two methods.

As no methylene blue was detected in the fish sample, standard addition method was employed to evaluate the practical applicability of R-CDs for methylene blue detection in fish samples. For fish samples (Brought from the local supermarket) preparation, thoroughly grated 5 g of fish until homogenized. Then, adding 500 mL water to the homogeneous fish. Next, the fish homogenate solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 30 min, and the insoluble impurities were removed by 0.45 μm filter membrane. Following, the standard solutions with different concentrations of methylene blue were added into the fish homogenate solution, and the fluorescence intensities of the standard solutions were measured by fluorescence spectrometer, and the standard curve was made. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of methylene blue in 0.80, 0.60 and 0.40 μM of fish homogenate solutions was determined by fluorescence spectrometer.Under the same experimental conditions, the experiment was repeated six times. HPLC method was also established and the standard curve was drawn for methylene blue. The steps of HPLC analysis and subsequent comparison with fluorescence method were consistent with those described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of R-CDs/PVA composite gel and preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of R-CDs-PVA composite gel was accorded to previous reported method with some modification [30]. In details, 1 mL of R-CDs water solution (1 mg·mL−1) was mixed with 5 mL of 20% polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-water solution. Then, the mixture was heated and stirred slowly until a homogeneous R-CDs/PVA gel solution was obtained. After that, the solution was added into a heart shape mould and air dried for 24 h. Finally, the R-CDs/PVA composite gel was obtained after demoulding. Besides, the R-CDs/PVA composite gel was employed to the preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes. The specific steps were: The gel was placed in water containing dye and pure water, respectively, and the fluorescence changes of the gel were observed with a portable UV lamp (365 nm).

By using melanoma cells, MTT measurement was conducted to investigate the cytotoxicity of the R-CDs. For details, cells were firstly seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for one day. Thereafter, different concentrations (10 μg·mL−1-1000 μg·mL−1) of R-CDs were added into the wells and incubated for another 24 h. Next, 10 μL of MTT with the concentration of 100 μg·mL−1 was separately added into each well and another 4 h incubation was conducted. The solutions in all wells were then removed and subsequently adding 150 μL of DMSO into each well to dissolve the formazan complex. Finally, after shaking the plates for 10 min, the absorbance was detected at 490 nm by use of a microplate reader.

For cell imaging, melanoma cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 containing penicillin–streptomycin and 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS).Cells were seeded in six-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Following, 500 μg·mL−1 of R-CDs solution was added into each well and placed into the medium for further culturing for 4 h.Finally, the cells were washed twice with phosphate buffered saline (PBS). All the imaging were captured by using fluorescent inverted microscope.
The preparation and application of R-CDs were shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to characterize the morphology of R-CDs, TEM characterization was done. As shown in Fig. 1
A, R-CDs were uniform spherical morphology and well-distributed with an average diameter size of 5.05 nm. R-CDs demonstrated obviously lattice fringes, for which the lattice-fringe spacing was calculated as 0.21 nm. This result revealed that in-plane lattice spacing of the (100) facet of graphite existed in R-CDs and R-CDs showed a graphite-like structure [31]. Further, XPS and FT-IR were used to study the chemical compositions of R-CDs. In the full scan spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1 B), four typical peaks at 284.77 eV, 399.49 eV, 531.9 eV and 167.81 eV originated from C1s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p energy levels clearly illustrated the successful doping of N and S into the structure of R-CDs.The contents of them were separately calculated as 80.47%, 8.75%, 9.63% and 1.16%. Specifically, the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 C) had two significant peaks for C = C/C-C, C-N/C-O/C-S chemical bonds at 284.8 eV and 285.9 eV, respectively [32]. The N1s spectra (Fig. 1 D) revealed two peaks at 399.3 eV and 400.3 eV, which can be ascribed to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [33,34]. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 1 E) were deconvoluted into two main peaks at 531.64 eV and 532.2 eV, demonstrating that R-CDs contained C = O and C-OH/C-O-C [35,36]. Similarly, the S 2p spectra also can be splitted into two main bands at 167.9 eV and 168.3 eV (Fig. 1 F), which could be an association with C-SO4-C and S = O, respectively [37,38]. Subsequently, FT-IR was used to identify the functional groups of R-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1 G, the characteristic peaks of FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs at 3450.65 cm−1, 3377.35 cm−1 and 3034.05 cm−1 were assigned to the O-H, N-H and unsaturation C-H stretching vibrations [39,40]. The peaks at 1699.28 cm−1, 1610.56 cm−1 and 1502.54 cm−1 were separately ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C = N and C = C. The absorption peaks at 1305.80 cm−1, 1128.35 cm−1, 754.16 cm−1 and 624.93 cm−1 were individually attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C-N=, C-N/C-O, S-O and C-S [35,41]. Based on the results in XPS and FT-IR characterization, it can be known that the R-CDs possessed multiple functional groups such as hydroxyl, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, carbonyl and -C-SO4 [42], which would bring surface molecular state related fluorescence, resulting in improving the possibility of luminescence and redshift for the emission wavelength of R-CDs, and also increasing the hydrophilicity and stability of R-CDs.

The optical characteristics of R-CDs were achieved. As shown in Fig. 2
A, the distinct absorption at 230 nm was assigned to the π-π* transition electron transition of C = N and C = C. The absorption peak at 278 nm associated with n-π* transition of the C = O band were also observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs. Besides, R-CDs also showed an absorption band around 600–700 nm, and the maximum absorption peak was at about 640 nm. Ascribed to the local recombination of surface energy and excited state energy, the absorption peak directly generated strong red fluorescence emission (600–700 nm) [33]. So to speak, the appearance of absorption peak at 640 nm was an important condition for red fluorescence emission. In addition, the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2 B indicated that the maximum emission wavelength of R-CDs was at 640 nm with an excitation wavelength of 580 nm. With the change of excitation wavelength, the position of maximum emission wavelength remained unshifted. This phenomenon might be resulted in the uniform surface states and narrow size distribution of R-CDs. Meanwhile, as can be seen in inset of Fig. 2 B, aqueous solution of the R-CDs was colorless and transparent, while it emitted bright red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of R-CDs to three food dyes
ce:section.ce:para:
To improve the detection performance of R-CDs to three food dyes and eliminate possible interferences in the subsequent actual sample analysis, several factors including the concentration of R-CDs, ionic strength, the incubation time, the stability of R-CDs, the influence of different metals and different dyes, were investigated at first. The fluorescence response intensity (F0/F or F/F0) was employed to evaluate the ability of three food dyes to quench the fluorescence of R-CDs (F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of R-CDs in the absence and presence of three food dyes, respectively). As shown in Fig. 2 C-2E, the best detection condition of R-CDs for three dyes was at the concentration of 25 μg·mL−1 without adding NaCl for incubation of 30 s. Besides, the stability of R-CDs was conducted by separately measuring the changes of fluorescence intensity within 10 h and 7 days. It can be obviously seen from Figure S3
A-S3B that R-CDs showed satisfactory stability. Further, the influence of different metals and different dyes on the fluorescence of R-CDs were also explored. As shown in Fig. 2 F and Figure S3
C, metal ions had little effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs, indicating that the R-CDs can be used for the analysis of target dyes in actual samples containing metal ions. What's more, as displayed in Fig. 2 G and Figure S3
D, except for amaranth, bright blue FCF and methylene blue, other dyes showed little quenching effects on the fluorescence of R-CDs, revealing the selectivity of R-CDs to three selected dyes. In addition, 10 μM of three selected dyes were separately added to a solution of R-CDs which contained other metal ions (1 mM) or dyes (10 μM) to further appraise the selectivity of R-CDs for three dyes. The results in Fig. 3
A-3F and Figure S4
A-S4F revealed that other metal ions or dyes showed little effects on the quenching effect of selected dyes to R-CDs, demonstrating that R-CDs showed high selectivity and good anti-interference performance.

Further, a series of fluorescent measurements with different concentrations of amaranth, bright blue FCF and methylene blue were carried out to explore the sensitivity of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4
A–4C, the fluorescence emission intensities of R-CDs under the exciting wavelength at 580 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of three dyes (0.2 μM-100 μM for amaranth, 10 nM-10 μM for bright blue FCF and 60 nM-20 μM for methylene blue). The values of the relative fluorescent intensities (F0/F) exhibited good linear relationships [F0/F = 0.1371C + 1.0239, F0/F = 0.3329C + 1.0776 and F0/F = 0.1597C + 0.9904 (R2 = 0.9970, 0.9930 and 0.9957)] versus the concentrations of amaranth, bright blue FCF and methylene blue in the ranges of 0.20 μM −20 μM, 10 nM-1 μM and 60 nM-8 μM, respectively (Shown in Fig. 4 D-4F). The LODs of them were separately determined as 70 nM, 4 nM and 20 nM based on the formula of 3σ/slope (σ represented the standard deviation of the blank solution, and the slope was the slope of the calibration curve). In comparison the linear ranges and LODs in present study with previous reported literatures (Shown in Table S1 in Supporting Material
s), it can be clearly seen that R-CDs was not only the first probe that can be used for sensitive detection of three dyes, but also had satisfactory sensitivity to three dyes. The results showed that R-CDs had the potential for the sensitive detection of three dyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the fluorescence quenching mechanisms for the detection of three dyes
ce:section.ce:para:
As reported, inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET), static quenching and dynamic quenching are the main quenching mechanisms. Moreover, if the quenching mechanism contains FRET, PET and dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs with and without quenchers will change [43]. Thus, to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanisms for the detection of three dyes, the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs with and without three dyes were investigated. Noticeably, as described in Fig. 5
A-C, the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs with and without three dyes almost had no difference, revealing that the quenching mechanisms for the detection of three dyes were not FRET, PET and dynamic quenching but may be IFE or static quenching. Besides, it is well-known that if the IFE occur, the adsorption band of an absorber has adequately the spectral overlap with the excitation and/or emission bands of a fluorophore. As can be known from Figure S5
A-S5C, the UV–vis absorption bands of three dyes overlapped with the excitation and /or emission wavelengths of R-CDs, indicating that the IFE was one of the fluorescence quenching mechanisms. Further, to explore whether the static quenching mechanism existed or not, the Stern-Volmer equation, for which the equation was shown in eq (1) below, was employed.
(1)
F 0 / F = 1 + K s v [ c ] = 1 + K q τ 0 [ c ]
where τ0 was the average lifetime of R-CDs. Kq was the quenching rate constant. Ksv referred to the quenching constant. Kq and Ksv were obtained by conducting the detection at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 5 D-5F and Table S2, the Ksv decreased with the increase of the temperature. Furthermore, the Kq calculated ranged from 3.11 × 1013 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 to 1.67 × 1015 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1 between 20℃ to 40℃ (the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was 2.209 ns) was much larger than the biggest Kq of collisional quenching (1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1). These phenomena revealed that the static quenching may be another fluorescence quenching mechanism [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of three selected dyes in real samples
Detection of amaranth, bright blue FCF in beverages
ce:section.ce:para:
As reported, amaranth and bright blue FCF are widely used as the food colorants in beverages. However, they can cause serious health problems for all living systems, so the levels of them in food and drink should be measured and their use in food should be controlled [12,45]. Herein, to evaluate the practicability and accuracy of the as-prepared R-CDs for the detection of these two dyes, watermelon flavored soda drink which mainly contained amaranth, and blue cocktail which mainly contained bright blue FCF were employed as the real samples. The concentrations of amaranth in watermelon flavored soda drink and bright blue FCF in blue cocktail were detected. As displayed in Table 1 , the concentration of amaranth in watermelon flavored soda drink was detected as 1.15 ± 0.09 (μM), while the concentration of bright blue FCF in blue cocktail was 2.57 ± 0.09 (μM). These two data were much lower than the maximum limit of two dyes in food, revealing that the amounts of these two dyes added to the two beverages were acceptable. Moreover, in order to further verify the accuracy of the fluorescence method, HPLC method was employed to determine the concentrations of amaranth and bright blue FCF in two beverages (HPLC analytical method was shown in Supporting Material
s). As shown in Table 1 and Fig. 6
A-6B, the concentrations of amaranth and bright blue FCF were separately determined as 1.17 ± 0.04 (μM) and 2.53 ± 0.06 (μM), which were highly consistent with those results of fluorescence detection method, further indicating the good accuracy of fluorescence detection method. These all results showed that R-CDs based fluorescence detection method had great potential for accurate analysis of amaranth and bright blue FCF in complex food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of methylene blue in real fish sample
ce:section.ce:para:
As well as we know, methylene blue is a thiazide dye widely used in chemical indicators and biological staining. It has a good effect on the prevention and control of wheel worm disease, small melon worm disease, inclined tube worm disease and so on. Therefore, it is often allowed to be used in limited quantities in aquaculture and transportation. However, in the absence of scientific guidance, in order to pursue the effect of sterilization or preservation, many farmers often use excessive methylene blue, which leads to consumers buying aquatic products especially fish with excessive methylene blue residue, thus causing harm to the body [46]. Thus, it is of great significance to detect methylene blue content in fish. Herein, the concentration of methylene blue in real fish sample was detected by using the developed fluorescence detection method. Since no methylene blue was detected in the purchased fish samples, a standard addition method was used to verify the validity of the method so as to subsequent detection of fish samples containing methylene blue. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of methylene blue ranged from 98.50% to 103.66% with the RSDs of 1.02%- 7.01%. The data showed that the detection process had good accuracy, and R-CDs can be applied to the detection of methylene blue in real fish samples. In addition, in order to verify the practicality of fluorescence detection method, the content of methylene blue in labeled fish samples was detected by HPLC (HPLC analytical method was shown in Supporting Material
s). The results were shown Table 3 . The same samples were detected by HPLC, and the detection results were consistent with those of fluorescence detection, indicating that the fluorescence detection method constructed can be used for the detection of methylene blue in fish samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes based on R-CDs/ PVA composite gel
ce:section.ce:para:
Because the as-prepared R-CDs was a kind of aggregated quenched CDs, the fluorescence would quench at high concentration. In order to be able to use R-CDs better. The R-CDs was prepared into a gel by combined with PVA. By distributing R-CDs in a PVA mesh, R-CDs can be separated by a PVA mesh to reduce or avoid π-π stacking and thus reduce the likelihood of fluorescence quenching. As showed in Fig. 7
A-7B, the as-prepared R-CDs/ PVA composite gel had a good molding effect. The R-CDs/PVA composite gel showed as a homogeneous light blue transparent gel under sunlight and emitted a bright red fluorescence under the UV lamp, indicating that the fluorescence characteristics of R-CDs were retained in the gel. Furthermore, the preliminary visualization analysis of three dyes by using the as-prepared gel was done. As displayed in Fig. 7C-7F, the gel which treated with water for 10 min still kept the bright red fluorescence under the UV lamp. However, the red fluorescence of gels which separately treated with three dyes for 10 min weakened, demonstrating that dyes may adsorb on gels and partly quench the fluorescence of R-CDs. Therefore, by putting gel into the actual sample which contained the selected dyes and directly observed the change of gel fluorescence on site, we can preliminarily determine whether there were dyes in the actual sample or not. The development of this gel provided a new mean and idea for the subsequent visual detection and adsorption of dyes, which can be further studied.

Because of the outstanding advantages including low photodamage and high tissue penetration of R-CDs, they showed unique superiority in cell imaging. Since the size of the R-CDs was less than 10 nm, they can be mediated by endocytosis such as lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, micropinocytosis, and caveolae-mediated endocytosis to enter into cells [47], and thus aggregating in cells. Since the aggregation process was slow and the quenching time was long, the fluorescence of CDs in cells can be monitored, so that CDs can be used for cell imaging. Before doing the cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of R-CDs was explored. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 8
A that in the concentration range of 10–1000 μg⋅mL−1, R-CDs showed no significant cytotoxicity, revealing the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of R-CDs, and they can be employed for cell imaging. Further, the potential of R-CDs for cell imaging had also been preliminatively explored. As displayed in Fig. 8 B-8C, after incubation with R-CDs for 4 h, the cytoplasm of cells emitted bright red fluorescence. This phenomenon indicated that the R-CDs can easily pass through the cell membrane and entering the cytoplasm. All these results revealed that R-CDs had great potential in bio-imaging applications.

In summary, N, S-codoped red emissive fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) with QY of 5.20% were successfully synthesized through a sulfuric acid-mediated hydrothermal synthesis method for the first time. R-CDs showed outstanding photostability, water solubility and fluorescence characteristics etc., thus, they can be applied as the fluorescence probe for the detection of three food dyes and for cell imaging. The quenching effects of dyes including amaranth, brilliant blue FCF and methylene blue showed good linear relationships and low LODs. Furthermore, in the analysis of actual beverage samples, the concentrations of amaranth and brilliant blue FCF detected by the constructed fluorescence method were highly consistent with the results of HPLC method, indicating the accuracy of the fluorescence method. Satisfactory recoveries (98.50%-103.66%) were obtained in the analysis of methylene blue in fish sample, and the results were also consistent with the results of HPLC method. Besides, the results of the cytotoxicity and cell imaging revealed that the R-CDs showed great potential in the field of biological imaging. As the R-CDs prepared were aggregative quenching type, R-CDs/PVA composite gel can be prepared by mixing R-CDs with PVA, which can make R-CDs still maintain good fluorescence performance under large concentration, and the prepared gel can be initially used for visual analysis of three dyes.

Meng Tian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lijuan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Dandan Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Yongqing Tao: Data curation, Investigation. Luchun Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Junji Wang: Software, Investigation. Shaochi Liu: Software, Investigation. Tian Quan: Software, Investigation. Famin Ke: Software, Investigation. Kailian Zhang: Software, Investigation. Xiang Li: Supervision, Writing – original draft. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121581.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Lett. 33(2) (2022) 903-906.
